patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 67-year-old retired school administrative assistant. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I’ve had this cough that won’t go away, and I keep bringing up a lot of mucus.” Volunteer: daily cough for months, worse in the mornings; thick mucus most days (sometimes yellow/green); get out of breath faster, especially uphill; told you have emphysema; two chest infections in the past year needing antibiotics. If asked, add: occasional wheeze/tight chest when sick, fatigue, low-grade fever/chills with infections, a few pounds of unintentional weight loss, and once or twice a small blood streak in mucus after hard coughing. Do not mention unless specifically asked: bad taste/bad breath after coughing up mucus, cough worse after lying down, needing to sit up to clear mucus, and rounded fingertips. Only admit urinary leakage with big coughing fits if asked directly. Mention lifelong sinus congestion/runny nose and frequent ear infections if asked about ENT/allergies. Express worry that nebulizer/airway-clearance supplies and frequent visits/imaging will be expensive; say you prefer to “wait until it gets bad.” If clinician shows empathy, thank them and become more open.

medications: Tiotropium Respimat 2.5 mcg 2 inhalations once daily
Albuterol HFA 90 mcg 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed for dyspnea/wheeze
Guaifenesin 600 mg by mouth twice daily as needed for sputum
Amlodipine 5 mg by mouth once daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg by mouth nightly
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU by mouth once daily
confounders: History of cigarette smoking (35 pack-years, quit 8 years ago) complicating separation of COPD/emphysema symptoms from bronchiectasis
Chronic gastroesophageal reflux with intermittent microaspiration contributing to cough/sputum and recurrent infections
Colonization with Pseudomonas aeruginosa which can mimic acute infection and drive chronic inflammation
Mild hypoxemia at baseline from emphysema can mask severity of bronchiectasis exacerbations

correct_diagnosis.primary: Idiopathic bronchiectasis
acceptable_alternatives: Bronchiectasis (non-cystic fibrosis)
Idiopathic non-CF bronchiectasis
required_evidence: History consistent with chronic airway disease and recurrent/persistent respiratory symptoms
High-resolution CT chest demonstrating bronchiectasis (airway dilation, lack of tapering, bronchial wall thickening) as the diagnostic standard
No identified secondary cause after appropriate evaluation (e.g., immunodeficiency, ABPA, aspiration, obstruction, prior severe infection)

Assessment for differentials when suggested by history/exam: primary ciliary dyskinesia (lifelong wet cough, chronic sinusitis/otitis, infertility, neonatal respiratory distress) and tracheobronchopathia osteochondroplastica (airway nodules sparing posterior membrane on bronchoscopy/CT)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal or mildly elevated if active infection/exacerbation; may be normal in stable disease
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be low if chronic inflammation or frequent illness
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal; may be mildly elevated as an acute phase reactant during inflammation
expected_lab_results.CRP: Normal when stable; elevated with acute exacerbation/infection
expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal to mildly elevated; can rise with chronic inflammation or exacerbation
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Creatinine: Typically normal; required to guide antibiotic dosing if used

medications: Albuterol 90 mcg/actuation inhaler 2 puffs inhaled every 4-6 hours as needed for wheeze or dyspnea
Azithromycin 500 mg orally three times weekly (e.g., Mon/Wed/Fri) for 6-12 months if frequent exacerbations (≥3/year) despite optimized airway clearance
non_pharmacological: Airway clearance therapy daily (e.g., active cycle of breathing, oscillatory PEP device) and increase frequency during exacerbations
Sputum culture when symptomatic/exacerbation to guide antibiotics; consider baseline culture if chronic sputum
Vaccinations: annual influenza and age/indication-appropriate pneumococcal vaccination
Pulmonary rehabilitation and regular aerobic exercise as tolerated
Smoking cessation and avoidance of inhaled irritants
Evaluate for underlying causes of bronchiectasis (immunoglobulins, ABPA testing, alpha-1 antitrypsin if emphysema disproportionate, and targeted evaluation for PCD if suggestive features)

Consider referral to pulmonology for HRCT confirmation, severity assessment, and long-term management; consider bronchoscopy if focal disease/obstruction suspected or to evaluate for tracheobronchopathia osteochondroplastica
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks after initiating airway clearance/bronchodilator (earlier in 48-72 hours if worsening symptoms); routine follow-up every 3-6 months once stable

must_include: Bronchiectasis means permanently widened/damaged airways that trap mucus and predispose to infections
Idiopathic means no clear cause found yet; further evaluation may still be needed to rule out treatable causes
Emphysema/COPD features can coexist and may contribute to shortness of breath
Airway clearance is the foundation of treatment and should be done regularly even when feeling well
Use rescue inhaler for episodic wheeze/dyspnea; demonstrate correct inhaler technique
Antibiotics are used when there is an exacerbation or, in select patients, as preventive therapy (e.g., azithromycin) after weighing benefits/risks
Review medication allergies: avoid penicillins due to urticarial rash; document sulfonamide intolerance (nausea/vomiting) and discuss alternatives
Discuss warning signs needing urgent care: increasing breathlessness, hemoptysis, high fever, chest pain, confusion, or low oxygen symptoms
Set expectations: goal is fewer infections, better breathing, and slower progression; condition is usually chronic
Smoking cessation and avoidance of secondhand smoke/irritants
Vaccination importance to prevent respiratory infections
Hydration, exercise/pulmonary rehab, and adherence to airway clearance routine
When to collect/bring sputum sample and why cultures matter for targeted therapy

Arrange follow-up to review culture results, assess response, reinforce airway clearance, and consider further evaluation for treatable causes if not already completed.
Close encounter after safety-netting and follow-up plan are in place.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious but clear-speaking accounting manager. Start by saying: “I’ve had this burning, electric pain deep in my pelvis and around my private area, especially when I sit.” Volunteer: worse with sitting, better standing/lying; feels like sitting on a small rock/rolled-up sock; months-long and wearing you down. If asked, add: spreads toward anus/rectum/genitals; numbness/pins-and-needles; painful sex and orgasm triggers flares; urinary urgency with little output; bowel movements spike pain after; better in the morning, builds through the day. Don’t mention unless specifically asked: leaning/perching to avoid sitting, clenched buttocks/inner thighs, long car rides trigger flares, donut cushion sometimes worse. Be reluctant/embarrassed unless asked gently: avoiding intimacy, occasional small urine leakage during intense pain, fear it’s “dirty” or “in your head.” Misconceptions to express: thought UTI but tests normal; wondered hemorrhoids; blamed ALS/“nerve problems” or too much sitting. Ask about costs/coverage and time off work. If clinician is empathetic, soften, thank them, and share the embarrassing details.

medications: Riluzole 50 mg twice daily
Dextromethorphan/quinidine 20 mg/10 mg twice daily
Baclofen 10 mg three times daily
Gabapentin 300 mg at bedtime
Ibuprofen 400 mg every 8 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Pelvic floor muscle hypertonicity/myofascial pelvic pain
History of prolonged sitting due to ALS-related mobility limitations
Chronic constipation (likely neurogenic and medication-related), worsening pelvic/perineal pain
Dyspareunia from genitourinary syndrome of menopause/perimenopausal vulvovaginal atrophy
Possible lumbosacral radiculopathy (overlapping neuropathic pain distribution)
Medication-related sedation/dizziness (gabapentin, baclofen) limiting exam tolerance and physical therapy participation

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pudendal neuralgia (pudendal nerve entrapment/irritation)
acceptable_alternatives: Chronic pelvic pain syndrome with suspected pudendal neuropathy
Neuropathic pelvic/perineal pain consistent with pudendal neuralgia
required_evidence: Neuropathic pain (burning/shooting/tingling) in pudendal nerve distribution (perineum, vulva/scrotum, anus, distal urethra)
Pain is worse with sitting and improves with standing/lying down
No objective sensory loss on exam (often normal neuro exam) or only mild allodynia in pudendal territory
No alternative explanation on focused evaluation (e.g., infection, mass, dermatologic lesion, radiculopathy, inflammatory bowel disease)
Reproduction of pain with palpation near ischial spine/Alcock canal or pelvic floor muscle tenderness (supportive)
Supportive response to diagnostic pudendal nerve block if performed (optional but supportive)

expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Normal renal function (baseline prior to neuropathic meds/NSAIDs)

medications: Gabapentin 100 mg PO at bedtime; increase by 100-300 mg every 3-7 days as tolerated toward 300 mg PO three times daily (adjust for renal function)
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower if liver disease/alcohol use)
non_pharmacological: Activity modification: avoid prolonged sitting; use a perineal cut-out (donut/ut-shaped) cushion; take standing breaks
Pelvic floor physical therapy focused on relaxation/down-training (avoid aggressive strengthening if it worsens pain)
Avoid triggers (cycling/rower, constipation/straining); bowel regimen as needed (fiber, hydration, stool softener) to reduce pelvic floor strain
Consider referral to pain medicine/urogynecology/urology/neurology for diagnostic pudendal nerve block and/or image-guided steroid/local anesthetic injection if persistent
Screen for and address comorbid anxiety/depression/sleep disturbance; consider CBT-based chronic pain strategies
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 2-4 weeks to assess response/titration and adverse effects; sooner (within 48-72 hours) if worsening neurologic symptoms, urinary retention/incontinence, fever, or new severe symptoms

must_include: Pudendal neuralgia is nerve-related pain in the pelvic/perineal region, often worse with sitting
Diagnosis is clinical and based on symptom pattern and exclusion of other causes; tests may be used to rule out alternatives
Red flags that would require urgent reassessment (fever, rapidly worsening symptoms, new weakness/numbness, bowel/bladder dysfunction)
Goal is pain reduction and functional improvement; treatment often requires a stepwise, multimodal approach
How to take and safely titrate gabapentin; common side effects (sleepiness, dizziness) and cautions (driving, alcohol/sedatives)
Expected timeframe for benefit (days to weeks) and plan to adjust if not improving
Validate impact of chronic pelvic pain and discuss prognosis (often improves with targeted therapy but may take time)
Discuss options if inadequate relief (alternate neuropathic agents, topical therapy, nerve blocks, specialist referral)
Shared decision-making around risks/benefits and patient preferences
Sitting strategies and cushion use; avoid cycling/prolonged sitting during flare
Constipation prevention to reduce pelvic floor strain
Importance of pelvic floor PT and adherence to home program

correct_diagnosis.primary: Japanese encephalitis

required_evidence: Acute febrile illness with altered mental status and/or seizures consistent with encephalitis

No better alternative explanation (e.g., hypoglycemia, intoxication, primary psychiatric disorder)
Supportive testing plan: lumbar puncture for CSF (lymphocytic pleocytosis, mildly elevated protein) and JE virus IgM in CSF/serum (or PCR early), plus neuroimaging/EEG as indicated

expected_lab_results.CBC - Neutrophils: Often normal or mildly increased early; not markedly neutrophilic as in typical bacterial meningitis

medications: Normal saline 0.9% IV titrate to euvolemia (avoid hypotonic fluids if hyponatremic)

Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV every 8 hours (use actual/adjusted body weight per protocol; renal dose adjust) until HSV encephalitis excluded
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV every 12 hours (empiric meningitis coverage) until bacterial meningitis excluded
Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV every 8-12 hours (target AUC per protocol) until bacterial meningitis excluded
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV every 6 hours (give with or just before first antibiotic dose; discontinue if bacterial meningitis unlikely)
non_pharmacological: Admit to hospital; ICU if airway compromise, refractory seizures, shock, or rising intracranial pressure concern
Airway/breathing/circulation stabilization; continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring
Frequent neurologic checks; seizure precautions
Evaluate and manage raised intracranial pressure as clinically indicated (head of bed 30°, avoid hyperthermia, consider hyperosmolar therapy per ICU protocol if signs of herniation/ICP)

Diagnostic steps: urgent lumbar puncture if no contraindication; brain imaging prior to LP if focal deficits, papilledema, severe immunocompromise, new-onset seizure, or markedly depressed consciousness
Infection control and public health: notify local public health as required; counsel on mosquito avoidance for household/community
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 hours for stabilization; daily inpatient review with de-escalation of empiric antimicrobials once CSF/serology results return (typically 24-72 hours); outpatient neurology/infectious disease follow-up 2-4 weeks after discharge for neurocognitive sequelae

Discuss expected course and prognosis variability; risk of neurologic complications and need for rehabilitation

comorbidities: Hereditary stomatocytosis (hemolytic anemia due to RBC membrane cation leak)
Hemolytic anemia due to erythrocyte adenosine deaminase overproduction
confounders: Recent viral upper respiratory infection (can worsen hemolysis and fatigue)
Oral contraceptive use (increases baseline venous thromboembolism risk; hereditary stomatocytosis can be associated with thrombosis, especially post-splenectomy)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary stomatocytosis (RBC membrane disorder causing chronic hemolytic anemia)
acceptable_alternatives: Dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis (xerocytosis)
Overhydrated hereditary stomatocytosis
required_evidence: CBC pattern consistent with hemolytic anemia (low hemoglobin/hematocrit with reticulocytosis)
MCV often elevated (macrocytosis) with relatively increased MCHC (more typical in dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis/xerocytosis) or low/normal MCHC (overhydrated form)
RDW often increased due to reticulocytosis/anisocytosis
Chronic, non-immune hemolysis suggested by elevated reticulocyte count/absolute reticulocytes without another CBC-based explanation
History consistent with inherited hemolytic anemia (lifelong anemia/jaundice, family history) and/or prior smear showing stomatocytes (if available outside current labs)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Decreased (mild to moderate anemia; may be near-normal in compensated cases)
expected_lab_results.RBC count: Decreased or low-normal (often reduced relative to degree of anemia due to macrocytosis)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Increased (macrocytosis; can be markedly elevated in overhydrated forms)
expected_lab_results.MCHC: Normal to increased (classically increased in dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis/xerocytosis; may be low/normal in overhydrated form)

non_pharmacological: Avoid splenectomy unless explicitly directed by hematology (splenectomy is associated with increased thromboembolic risk in hereditary stomatocytosis, particularly xerocytosis)
Refer to hematology for confirmation (membrane studies/genetic testing) and longitudinal management
Assess for and manage triggers of hemolysis/anemia (intercurrent infection, dehydration); maintain adequate hydration
If symptomatic or severe anemia: arrange urgent evaluation for transfusion per institutional thresholds and symptoms (hematology-directed)
Counsel on potential complications (gallstones, iron overload if transfused) and need for monitoring
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 2-4 weeks (earlier if symptomatic anemia, rapid decline, or transfusion needs)

must_include: This is an inherited red blood cell membrane condition that can cause chronic hemolysis and anemia
The available blood counts suggest hemolysis (anemia with increased reticulocytes) and macrocytosis pattern is common
Confirmatory testing and specialist input are needed because management differs from other hemolytic anemias
Folic acid supplementation supports red blood cell production in chronic hemolysis
Splenectomy is generally avoided in hereditary stomatocytosis due to higher clot risk; decisions should be hematology-led

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy (HMSN)
CMT1A (PMP22 duplication) if genetic subtype specified
required_evidence: Chronic, slowly progressive distal symmetric weakness/atrophy (especially feet/lower legs) with sensory loss
Foot deformities (pes cavus, hammertoes) and/or frequent ankle sprains/foot drop
Reduced/absent distal reflexes
Family history suggestive of inherited neuropathy (often autosomal dominant)
Electrodiagnostic testing consistent with peripheral neuropathy (demyelinating with slowed NCV or axonal with reduced amplitudes) and/or confirmatory genetic testing
No acute systemic illness or medication trigger to explain symptoms; CK elevation (if present) is mild and not the primary driver

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to mildly elevated (often <2–3x upper limit of normal); marked elevation would prompt evaluation for primary myopathy/rhabdomyolysis instead of CMT
expected_lab_results.Aldolase: Normal (or mildly elevated if CK mildly elevated); significant elevation suggests primary myopathy
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal or mildly elevated if CK elevated (muscle source possible); large isolated rise would suggest hepatic or severe muscle injury
expected_lab_results.ALT: Usually normal; mild elevation can occur but disproportionate ALT elevation suggests hepatic source
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Normal unless dehydration/renal disease; elevated with rhabdomyolysis or CKD
expected_lab_results.eGFR: Normal unless underlying kidney disease; decreased if renal impairment
expected_lab_results.BUN: Normal; can be elevated with dehydration/renal impairment

medications: No disease-modifying medication indicated for Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease; avoid empiric steroids/immunotherapy unless an acquired neuropathy is diagnosed
If neuropathic pain present: Gabapentin 100-300 mg PO at bedtime, then titrate to 300 mg PO TID as tolerated (renal dose adjust)
If neuropathic pain alternative: Duloxetine 30 mg PO daily, increase to 60 mg PO daily as tolerated (avoid in severe hepatic disease)
non_pharmacological: Referral to neurology for confirmation (EMG/NCS) and genetic counseling/testing as appropriate
Physical therapy for strengthening, stretching (Achilles/plantar fascia), balance and gait training
Occupational therapy for hand weakness and adaptive devices
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFO) for foot drop; custom orthotics for pes cavus; supportive footwear
Fall-risk assessment and home safety modifications
Podiatry/orthopedics referral for progressive deformity, recurrent ulcers, or consideration of corrective surgery
Screen for and manage complications: ankle instability, plantar pressure injuries, scoliosis (if indicated)
If CK is elevated: review exercise, trauma, alcohol, supplements, and myotoxic drugs; ensure hydration and repeat CK if clinically appropriate
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks (earlier if worsening weakness, new bulbar/respiratory symptoms, dark urine, or rapidly rising CK); neurology follow-up after EMG/NCS results

It is typically chronic and not contagious; progression varies by subtype

No cure currently; treatment focuses on function, safety, and symptom control
Role of PT/OT and braces/orthotics to improve gait and prevent falls

Discuss inheritance patterns and offer family planning/genetic counseling options
Set expectations about prognosis and monitoring for progression

TSH low or inappropriately normal in the setting of symptoms/signs consistent with central hypothyroidism (if present); interpret with clinical context

expected_lab_results.Sodium: Low-normal to low (hyponatremia can occur in secondary adrenal insufficiency)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal (hyperkalemia is uncommon in secondary adrenal insufficiency)
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: Low (consistent with hypotonic hyponatremia if sodium is low)
expected_lab_results.Glucose (fasting): Low-normal to low (hypoglycemia can occur with adrenal insufficiency)
expected_lab_results.8 AM serum cortisol: Low (below reference range for morning cortisol)
expected_lab_results.ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal (not elevated despite low cortisol)
expected_lab_results.Cosyntropin stimulation test (250 mcg) -: Blunted/inadequate rise; peak below lab threshold for normal adrenal reserve (commonly <18 mcg/dL [500 nmol/L])
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal (suggesting central hypothyroidism if free T4 were low; TSH alone may be non-diagnostic)

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old mom bringing your young daughter. Be cooperative and speak clearly, worried but organized. Start with: “She’s been losing skills—she hardly uses her hands like she used to.” Early in the visit, volunteer: she used to say a few words but now mostly doesn’t talk or understand; repetitive hand-wringing/rubbing; meals take a long time with coughing/gagging; walking is unsteady and stiff/awkward.

If asked about other symptoms, mention: frequent night waking/crying; odd breathing spells (holding breath or fast breathing when upset); constipation; anxiety/irritability that varies; she doesn’t use hands to play or feed herself though she can move them.

Do not mention unless specifically probed: brief “zoning out” spells, sound sensitivity, low pain response, slowed growth.

Resist/only admit if directly asked: teeth grinding and daytime wetting.

Express misconceptions: you wondered about autism or cerebral palsy.

If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and say you feel overwhelmed. Ask about costs/insurance coverage for swallow study, feeding therapy, specialists, supplements, and adaptive equipment.

Systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (if quotidian fevers/systemic features are present)
required_evidence: Chronic inflammatory arthritis with joint swelling and/or limitation of motion for >6 weeks in a child/adolescent
Evidence of inflammation (elevated ESR and/or CRP) during active disease
Exclusion of septic arthritis and malignancy (no acute toxic appearance, no severe leukocytosis with blasts, no focal bone pain out of proportion)
Known pathogenic LACC1 variant OR strong clinical phenotype consistent with LACC1-associated juvenile arthritis (early onset, refractory course, polyarticular involvement)
CBC may show anemia of inflammation; iron studies consistent with inflammation (low serum iron with normal/high ferritin)

expected_lab_results.ESR: Elevated during active synovitis (often >20–30 mm/hr; may be higher with systemic inflammation)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low to mildly low consistent with anemia of chronic inflammation
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal or mildly low (often normocytic; can be borderline microcytic if prolonged inflammation/functional iron deficiency)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (inflammatory leukocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal to elevated (acute-phase reactant; elevated with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Serum iron: Low (functional iron deficiency/anemia of inflammation pattern)

medications: Dexibuprofen 5-10 mg/kg PO every 8 hours with food (max per local pediatric dosing limits)
Methotrexate 10-15 mg/m^2 PO or SC once weekly (start after baseline assessment; adjust to response/tolerance)
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily (or 5 mg PO once weekly on a different day than methotrexate)
non_pharmacological: Pediatric rheumatology referral/ongoing co-management
Physical therapy and occupational therapy for range of motion, strengthening, and function; joint protection strategies
Screen for and refer to ophthalmology for uveitis per JIA protocol (baseline and periodic slit-lamp exams)
Vaccination review: ensure routine immunizations; avoid live vaccines if later placed on significant immunosuppression per specialist guidance
Use heat/cold, activity pacing, and school accommodations as needed
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks for symptom control and NSAID tolerance; if methotrexate started, clinical follow-up in 4-8 weeks and labs per protocol; urgent review sooner for fever, severe pain, or functional decline

How to take methotrexate weekly (not daily) and take folic acid to reduce side effects
Key side effects to report: mouth sores, significant nausea/vomiting, jaundice/dark urine, unusual bruising/bleeding, severe infection symptoms

Discuss expected timeline for improvement and need for regular monitoring

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms (pain, stiffness, swelling, function), flare pattern, systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss), red flags (severe infection), and medication history to confirm appropriateness of planned therapy.

Screen for extra-articular disease and monitoring needs: eye symptoms (pain, redness, photophobia, blurred vision) and prior ophthalmology screening for uveitis; review immunization status and infection exposure relevant to immunosuppression.

Required safety check before prescribing: confirm and document amoxicillin-clavulanate allergy (urticarial rash), clarify severity, timing, and any anaphylaxis features; check for prior NSAID or methotrexate reactions.

Baseline monitoring prior to methotrexate: CBC with differential, AST/ALT, albumin, creatinine/BUN; consider hepatitis B/C and TB screening per local rheumatology protocol if escalation to biologic therapy is anticipated.
Ensure baseline labs are acceptable for methotrexate initiation and to set a monitoring schedule.
Confirm juvenile arthritis due to LACC1 defect and summarize disease nature, prognosis, and key complications (including uveitis risk).

Non-critical safety check before prescribing: review current meds/supplements for interactions (e.g., other hepatotoxins; high-dose NSAIDs; trimethoprim-containing antibiotics) and verify no duplicate NSAID therapy.
Prescribe anti-inflammatory symptom control (dexibuprofen) and disease-modifying therapy (methotrexate weekly + folic acid), with clear dosing frequency and monitoring plan.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment rationale, dosing (weekly MTX), expected timeline, side effects and urgent return precautions; NSAID safety; emphasize uveitis screening.
Arrange rheumatology follow-up and lab monitoring schedule; ensure ophthalmology screening plan for uveitis; plan reassessment of response and escalation if inadequate.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, answering questions, and documenting shared decision-making.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 47-year-old man, self-employed auto body tech. You are vague and a bit evasive. Start with: “I keep getting these scary episodes where my heart races and I can’t catch my breath.” Volunteer only: sudden pounding fast heartbeat, shortness of breath during spells, chest tightness, shakiness/on edge. Minimize severity and push for a quick fix; say you can’t miss work and don’t want “a big workup,” and that cash pay should make it one-and-done.

If asked directly about details, then share: peaks in minutes, lasts 10–30 min, sweating/hot, lightheaded, nausea, tingling, dread, exhausted afterward, avoids exercise/crowds, sometimes wakes at night, more headaches.

Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about meds/substances: heavy decongestant use, lots of coffee/energy drinks, poor sleep, weight loss/hunger, tremor, loose stools, neck fullness, family thyroid issues. Strongly resist admitting: taking someone else’s ADHD stimulant, recent cocaine, increased alcohol; only confess if asked nonjudgmentally and reassured.

Express misconceptions: smartwatch “proves” an arrhythmia; maybe asthma, low sugar, or a blood clot after a long flight.

If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but stay guarded; you fear being told it’s “just anxiety.”

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sepsis due to community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), likely bacterial
acceptable_alternatives: Severe community-acquired pneumonia without sepsis
Aspiration pneumonia with systemic inflammatory response
Acute bacterial bronchitis with dehydration and electrolyte derangement (less likely)
required_evidence: Systemic illness consistent with infection (fever or hypothermia, tachycardia, tachypnea, or hypotension) plus suspected pulmonary source (cough, dyspnea, pleuritic pain, abnormal lung exam or chest imaging if obtained)
CBC showing leukocytosis with neutrophilia (or leukopenia in severe cases)
Evidence of organ dysfunction or hypoperfusion consistent with sepsis (e.g., hypotension, altered mental status, elevated lactate if available, oliguria) or need for IV fluids/vasopressors
Electrolyte abnormalities consistent with acute illness (e.g., low sodium) and/or metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate) supporting severity

expected_lab_results.WBC: Elevated (e.g., 13-22 x10^9/L) or low in severe sepsis; left shift may be present
expected_lab_results.Neutrophils: Elevated absolute neutrophil count (neutrophilia) consistent with bacterial infection
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Often normal or mildly low (anemia of inflammation or dilutional if resuscitated)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal early; may be low in severe sepsis or evolving DIC
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Variable; may be normal or low if poor intake/diuresis; may rise with acidosis/renal dysfunction
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Often normal; may increase after normal saline resuscitation
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low or low-normal if metabolic acidosis from sepsis/hypoperfusion

medications: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV once daily
Azithromycin 500 mg IV/PO once daily
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO/PR every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain
non_pharmacological: Sepsis bundle: obtain blood cultures before antibiotics if this does not delay therapy, start broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour when sepsis is suspected
IV crystalloid resuscitation (e.g., 30 mL/kg) for hypotension or signs of hypoperfusion; reassess frequently
Supplemental oxygen to maintain adequate saturation; consider escalation of respiratory support if needed
Source evaluation: chest imaging (CXR) and sputum culture if feasible; consider viral testing when appropriate
Monitor vitals, urine output, mental status; repeat labs and reassess response to therapy
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 hours after initial interventions; daily clinical review and CBC/BMP trend while inpatient; de-escalate antibiotics at 48-72 hours based on cultures/clinical response

must_include: Concern for a serious lung infection and possible sepsis (body-wide response to infection)
Why urgent treatment is needed (risk of worsening breathing, low blood pressure, organ dysfunction)
What tests are being used to confirm source and severity (blood work, cultures, chest imaging if obtained)
Immediate antibiotics and supportive care (fluids, oxygen) and expected timeline for improvement
Plan to tailor/de-escalate antibiotics once culture results and clinical response are known
Potential side effects (GI upset, rash; azithromycin QT risk in susceptible patients) and to report breathing difficulty, swelling, or hives
Penicillin allergy history details (reaction type, severity, timing) and rationale for using ceftriaxone with low cross-reactivity in non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy
What to do if allergic symptoms occur (stop medication, seek urgent care)
Discuss need for hospitalization/monitoring if unstable
Hydration, rest, and avoiding smoking/vaping
Return precautions: worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, persistent fever, inability to keep fluids down
Importance of completing the prescribed antibiotic course and follow-up

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 47-year-old self-employed auto body shop owner with a vague, evasive style. Start by saying you keep having scary episodes where your heart suddenly pounds fast and you feel like you might faint. Volunteer: lightheadedness, shaky/sweaty, chest tightness during episodes, and that they’re happening more often over the last couple months. If asked directly, add: sometimes triggered by standing quickly or hot showers, short of breath, tingling fingers/dry mouth, wiped out for hours afterward, near-faints, increased thirst/salty cravings, and occasional loose stools.

Do NOT volunteer weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor at rest, gritty/light-sensitive eyes, or fuller-looking neck unless specifically asked about those.

Be resistant about stimulants: only admit energy drinks/coffee at first; deny “drugs.” If pressed nonjudgmentally, reveal fat-burner/pre-workout pills (sometimes extra doses) and increased nicotine pouches.

Express misconceptions: you think it’s panic attacks or low blood sugar; smartwatch says “possible irregular rhythm.” Worry about expensive tests, time off work, and insurance denying coverage. If the clinician shows empathy, soften slightly and share more details but still minimize costs/impact.

medications: Metformin 1000 mg PO twice daily
Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 40 mg PO nightly
Ibuprofen 600 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Recent NSAID use (ibuprofen) contributing to acute kidney injury risk and masking fever/pain
Poorly controlled diabetes increasing infection risk and blunting typical symptoms
Possible dehydration from reduced oral intake leading to prerenal azotemia and elevated lactate

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute upper gastrointestinal bleeding due to peptic ulcer disease (non-variceal GI bleed)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute gastrointestinal bleeding (source not yet localized)
Erosive gastritis with acute blood loss anemia
Mallory-Weiss tear
Lower gastrointestinal bleeding (to be ruled out if hematochezia predominates)
required_evidence: Clinical evidence of GI bleeding (hematemesis and/or melena; or hematochezia with hemodynamic instability suggesting brisk upper source)
Evidence of acute blood loss anemia (decreasing hemoglobin/hematocrit or symptomatic anemia)
Risk factors or history consistent with peptic ulcer disease (NSAID use, prior ulcer, H. pylori risk) OR absence of chronic liver disease stigmata making variceal bleed less likely
Need for urgent endoscopic evaluation for diagnosis/therapy (non-variceal bleed pathway)

expected_lab_results.WBC: May be mildly elevated due to stress response; can be normal
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Low or falling compared to baseline (may be initially normal early in acute bleed)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Usually normal unless comorbid thrombocytopenia; assess for bleeding risk
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often normal; may be low with volume depletion
expected_lab_results.Potassium: May be low with vomiting; can be normal
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): May be elevated with vomiting/metabolic alkalosis; may be low if lactic acidosis from hypoperfusion
expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated out of proportion to creatinine (not provided) consistent with upper GI bleed and/or volume depletion

medications: Pantoprazole 80 mg IV bolus once, then 8 mg/hour IV continuous infusion for 72 hours (or pantoprazole 40 mg IV q12h if infusion not feasible) pending endoscopy
Ondansetron 4 mg IV/PO q6-8h PRN nausea/vomiting
non_pharmacological: Immediate hemodynamic assessment; place two large-bore IVs; continuous vital sign monitoring
NPO; type and screen/crossmatch; prepare blood products as indicated
IV isotonic crystalloid resuscitation (e.g., lactated Ringer’s or normal saline) guided by vitals/mental status/urine output
Transfuse packed RBCs if hemoglobin ≤7 g/dL (≤8 g/dL if active ischemia/cardiovascular disease or ongoing hemodynamic instability)
Hold/avoid NSAIDs and other ulcerogenic agents; review and hold anticoagulants/antiplatelets when appropriate and coordinate reversal if life-threatening bleed
Urgent gastroenterology consult for endoscopy within 24 hours (sooner if ongoing bleeding/hemodynamic instability)
Risk stratify (e.g., Glasgow-Blatchford) to guide level of care; admit (often monitored bed/ICU if unstable)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Endoscopy within 24 hours; reassess vitals and hemoglobin every 6-8 hours initially (or more frequently if unstable), then outpatient follow-up 1-2 weeks after discharge for ulcer management/H. pylori testing and PPI continuation plan

must_include: Concern for bleeding in the gastrointestinal tract, most likely from an ulcer in the stomach/duodenum
Why this can be dangerous (risk of shock, anemia) and why urgent evaluation is needed
That endoscopy is the key test to locate and treat the bleeding source
Immediate stabilization steps (IV fluids, blood tests, possible transfusion)
Purpose of IV proton pump inhibitor therapy to reduce rebleeding risk
Plan for endoscopy and possible endoscopic therapy
Medication avoidance (no NSAIDs; review blood thinners) and when these may be restarted
Return precautions: recurrent vomiting blood, black/bloody stools, dizziness/syncope, chest pain, worsening weakness
Discuss transfusion risks/benefits if indicated
Clarify allergy history (TMP-SMX rash/hives) and confirm no reaction to planned meds
Avoid NSAIDs; use acetaminophen instead unless contraindicated
Limit alcohol and stop smoking if applicable to reduce ulcer risk
Need for H. pylori testing/treatment after stabilization and adherence to ulcer-healing regimen

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for suspected upper GI bleeding (hematemesis/melena, onset/volume, syncope, abdominal pain), hemodynamic risk, and key etiologies/med risks (NSAIDs, anticoagulants/antiplatelets, alcohol, liver disease).
Order initial stabilization and bleeding workup labs to assess severity and guide transfusion/reversal: CBC, CMP, PT/INR, aPTT, type & screen/crossmatch, lactate as indicated.
Critical safety check before prescribing (e.g., IV PPI, antiemetics, antibiotics if later needed). Clarify reported TMP-SMX rash/hives and confirm no allergy to planned meds.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing to identify interactions (e.g., PPIs with clopidogrel; anticoagulants/antiplatelets; QT-prolonging antiemetics).
Re-confirm allergy status immediately prior to any medication order if new meds are added (e.g., sedation-related meds later, antibiotics for H. pylori).
Initiate indicated acute therapy consistent with upper GI bleed pathway (e.g., IV PPI; antiemetic if needed) and explicitly avoid NSAIDs; include transfusion plan as contingent on Hb/hemodynamics.

Critical contraindication check before ordering definitive/procedural steps (e.g., endoscopy/sedation suitability, anticoagulation reversal considerations, pregnancy status when relevant, renal/hepatic constraints).
Order any additional tests needed based on history (e.g., repeat CBC, iron studies later; H. pylori testing deferred until stable; pregnancy test in those of childbearing potential).
Review labs to risk-stratify (anemia degree, INR elevation, renal function), decide on transfusion thresholds, and urgency of endoscopy/level of care.
Document working diagnosis: suspected upper GI bleed, likely peptic ulcer disease, with differential (variceal bleed, gastritis, Mallory-Weiss tear, malignancy) and severity assessment.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis dangers/urgency, endoscopy role, stabilization steps, PPI purpose, medication avoidance, and return precautions; discuss transfusion risks/benefits if relevant.
Arrange urgent evaluation pathway (ED/ICU as needed; GI consult/endoscopy timing) and after-stabilization follow-up for H. pylori testing/treatment and ulcer-healing regimen adherence.
Close encounter after ensuring safety-netting, clear plan, and documentation of critical safety checks.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 52-year-old cooperative, clear-speaking administrative assistant. Start with: “I can’t stop having watery diarrhea, and my belly keeps getting bigger.” Early in the visit, volunteer: watery diarrhea many times/day with urgency/incontinence risk, swollen tight belly “like fluid,” intense thirst/dryness, and intermittent crampy abdominal discomfort. If asked, add: unintentional weight loss, weakness after bowel movements, occasional muscle cramps/shaky legs, brief flushing episodes, decreased urination on bad days, and no blood in stool. Do not mention unless directly asked: lightheadedness on standing, racing heart when dehydrated, very dry mouth/cracked lips, and that you don’t get your usual heartburn anymore (“stomach acid feels low”). Be reluctant/embarrassed about accidents; admit only if asked sensitively. Offer misconceptions if the clinician is exploring causes: worry about allergy/angioedema belly swelling, tingling suggesting a genetic nerve disease, and pale watery stools meaning fat-malabsorption. Emphasize cost fears: ask to “do the minimum tests,” assume specialty/tumor testing and meds are unaffordable/generic not available, and delay referrals. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, share more details, and ask for a cost-conscious plan.

medications: Loperamide 2 mg PO after each loose stool PRN (max 16 mg/day)
Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily
Potassium chloride 40 mEq PO BID
Magnesium oxide 400 mg PO BID
confounders: Chronic proton pump inhibitor use (can mask/alter evaluation of acid-related symptoms and overlap with other secretory diarrhea etiologies)
Intermittent laxative (senna) use for prior constipation (can mimic secretory diarrhea if not disclosed)
Recent travel with self-treated diarrheal illness (raises concern for infectious gastroenteritis)
Ascites with low SAAG/high protein suggesting peritoneal malignancy, complicating attribution of symptoms to diarrhea-related volume depletion alone

correct_diagnosis.primary: VIPoma
acceptable_alternatives: Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor secreting VIP (WDHA syndrome)
required_evidence: Markedly elevated fasting plasma VIP (typically >200 pg/mL) in the appropriate clinical context
Electrolyte pattern consistent with secretory diarrhea: hypokalemia and non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate) with hyperchloremia
Clinical syndrome compatible with WDHA (watery diarrhea, hypokalemia, achlorhydria) even if not all features are explicitly reported
Ascites present; consider malignant/advanced neuroendocrine tumor or associated liver involvement (requires imaging confirmation outside provided labs)

expected_lab_results.VIP (vasoactive intestinal peptide), pla: Elevated, often markedly (classically >200 pg/mL fasting; may be much higher)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Low (hypokalemia) due to secretory GI losses
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low (non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis) from bicarbonate loss in stool
expected_lab_results.Chloride: High or high-normal (hyperchloremia) consistent with non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Low-normal to low (dehydration-related; may be normal depending on fluid status)
expected_lab_results.Calcium (total): Normal to mildly elevated (can be elevated; evaluate for MEN1 if hypercalcemia present)
expected_lab_results.Glucose (fasting): Elevated or high-normal (VIP can cause hyperglycemia)

medications: Octreotide 50 mcg subcutaneous every 8 hours (titrate to symptom control; consider long-acting formulation once stabilized)
Potassium chloride 20-40 mEq IV/PO (route and rate per severity; replete to goal K+ >4.0 mEq/L)
Magnesium sulfate 1-2 g IV (or magnesium oxide 400-800 mg PO daily) as needed to correct hypomagnesemia
0.9% sodium chloride IV bolus 500-1000 mL then maintenance per volume status (with ongoing losses replaced)
non_pharmacological: Assess volume status and replace ongoing stool/third-space losses; strict I&O and daily weights
Evaluate ascites (diagnostic paracentesis if clinically indicated) and manage with sodium restriction/diuretics only after stabilization and etiology assessment

Arrange tumor localization and staging (contrast CT/MRI abdomen/pelvis; somatostatin receptor imaging such as Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT) and refer to endocrinology/oncology/surgical oncology for definitive management

If resectable: surgical resection of primary tumor; if metastatic/unresectable: consider liver-directed therapy and systemic options (e.g., somatostatin analog continuation; targeted/PRRT per specialist)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1 week after stabilization (earlier/within 24-72 hours if inpatient) with repeat electrolytes and symptom assessment; oncology/surgery referral urgently (within 1-2 weeks) for staging and definitive therapy planning

must_include: VIPoma is a rare neuroendocrine tumor that can release VIP hormone
VIP excess causes large-volume secretory diarrhea and electrolyte losses (low potassium, low bicarbonate) leading to dehydration and weakness
Ascites may indicate advanced disease or liver involvement and needs further evaluation with imaging and possibly paracentesis
Immediate priorities are IV fluids and replacing potassium/magnesium to prevent dangerous heart rhythm problems
Octreotide reduces VIP secretion and improves diarrhea/electrolyte losses
Definitive management requires locating and staging the tumor and considering surgery or oncology-directed therapies
Review penicillin allergy (hives) and reassure that planned medications are not penicillins; confirm no prior reactions to similar drugs
Warn about red flags requiring urgent care: severe weakness, confusion, fainting, palpitations, inability to keep up with fluid losses, worsening abdominal distension or breathing difficulty
Maintain hydration with oral rehydration solutions when possible and track stool output
Avoid self-treating with large amounts of free water alone if ongoing diarrhea; prioritize electrolyte-containing fluids
Keep follow-up appointments for repeat labs and imaging; bring a list of all OTC meds/supplements

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old woman. You are cooperative, clear, and a bit worried. Start by saying: “My belly keeps hurting and I keep getting blocked up—I’ve been vomiting on and off.” Volunteer early: crampy wave-like belly pain mostly after eating, episodes of vomiting (sometimes green), bloating/tight abdomen, long history of constipation now worse. Also mention you were born with a split upper lip with childhood surgeries and have odd-looking fingers/toes since birth.

If asked, disclose: prior similar episodes told “possible intestinal blockage,” past abdominal surgeries for bowel problems, sometimes can’t pass gas during worst episodes, dry cracked lips/sores, patchy eyebrows/thin hair, small size/trouble gaining weight, headaches with light sensitivity, irregular periods.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: greasy/floating stools, mouth ulcers, choking on thin liquids, recurrent sinus/ear infections, streaky skin pigment, dental crowding/cavities.

Resist sharing at first: stool leakage when severely constipated and small blood on toilet paper—admit if asked gently.

Express misconceptions: suspect gluten/lactose, “food poisoning,” worry about appendicitis. If clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and share more. Bring up cost/insurance and fear of missing work when tests/referrals are discussed.

cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily

comorbidities: Curry-Jones syndrome (mosaic SMO-related disorder) with craniofacial anomalies and patchy skin hypoplasia
History of intestinal malrotation with intermittent obstructive symptoms
Chronic iron-deficiency anemia due to malabsorption and poor intake
Chronic constipation with episodic abdominal pain
confounders: Intermittent partial small-bowel obstruction from malrotation/adhesions can mimic infectious gastroenteritis or IBS
Chronic PPI use may worsen magnesium and iron absorption, complicating interpretation of malabsorption-related labs
Underweight BMI and low albumin can suggest eating disorder, masking gastrointestinal structural causes
Antiepileptic therapy and poor intake can contribute to fatigue/dizziness, overlapping with anemia symptoms
Patchy cutaneous findings may be misattributed to eczema or contact dermatitis, delaying recognition of a mosaic syndrome

correct_diagnosis.primary: Curry-Jones syndrome (CJS)
acceptable_alternatives: Iron deficiency anemia (comorbidity/complication)
Anemia of chronic inflammation (alternative explanation for iron studies)
Thalassemia trait (microcytosis differential)
Celiac disease with malabsorption (microcytic anemia differential)
Occult gastrointestinal blood loss (microcytic anemia differential)
required_evidence: Known or suspected Curry-Jones syndrome based on congenital pattern: craniosynostosis and/or craniofacial anomalies, polysyndactyly/syndactyly, gastrointestinal hamartomatous polyps/malrotation, and cutaneous findings consistent with mosaicism
Evidence of iron deficiency on iron studies (low ferritin and/or low serum iron with high TIBC) and/or microcytosis on CBC indices if anemia present
No alternative single diagnosis better explains the multisystem congenital findings (e.g., isolated thalassemia trait would not explain craniosynostosis + limb anomalies + GI polyposis)

non_pharmacological: Assess for and address sources of iron loss or malabsorption (history for GI bleeding, dietary intake; consider GI evaluation for polyps/bleeding as clinically indicated in CJS)

Coordinate multidisciplinary care appropriate to Curry-Jones syndrome (genetics, gastroenterology for hamartomatous polyps/bleeding surveillance, craniofacial/orthopedics/dermatology as indicated)

must_include: Curry-Jones syndrome is a rare congenital condition affecting multiple body systems; care is usually coordinated with specialists

general_concerns: Believes his balance problems (ataxia) are caused by being "lazy" or "not trying hard enough" and that exercise alone will cure it.
Thinks seizures (which he had as a child) are only triggered by stress and that if he stays calm he cannot have one, so follow-up is unnecessary.
Assumes his condition is contagious or something he "caught" because people sometimes stare at his facial features and coordination problems.
Believes the condition will inevitably get worse quickly and that he will lose the ability to walk in the next few years no matter what doctors do.
Thinks genetic conditions always come from the mother, and worries his mother is "to blame" for his problems.
economic_concerns: Worries that genetic testing and specialist visits will be unaffordable and assumes insurance will not cover anything "genetic."
Fears that getting a formal diagnosis in his chart will make him lose his job or disability benefits.
Believes therapy (PT/OT/behavioral support) is optional and not worth the cost because it "doesn’t fix the gene."

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 22-year-old man who is anxious and vague. Start with: “I keep stumbling and losing my balance, and my mom says I’m acting different.” Volunteer early: stumbling/bumping into things (worse when tired), mild hand shaking with fine tasks, more irritability/outbursts, lifelong learning problems, and that your mom says your face looks a bit different from siblings. If asked directly, add: occasional slurred/slower speech, headaches twice weekly, brief “staring spells,” more tripping on stairs/clumsy legs, sensory overwhelm with bright/loud, poor sleep/daytime fatigue, and that your dad had similar balance problems. Do NOT mention thirst/peeing/weight gain/blurry vision/constipation/planning/routines unless specifically asked about urination, thirst, weight, appetite, vision, bowel habits, or daily functioning. Resist admitting bedwetting, public anxiety, yelling/throwing things, self-scratching/hitting, and hiding zoning-out; only reveal if asked gently and specifically, and show embarrassment/fear (“I’m scared it’s seizures”). Express misconceptions: dehydration/diabetes/ear problem/energy drinks causing tremor/weight from inactivity. Worry about cost, insurance not covering “genetic,” and losing job/benefits; downplay PT/OT as “optional.” If clinician is empathic, you relax slightly, answer more, and ask if testing/therapy can be affordable and kept private.

required_evidence: History consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance (affected first-degree relative or documented de novo AD variant)
Global developmental delay/intellectual disability with behavioral abnormalities
Craniofacial dysmorphism on exam
Ataxia or gait incoordination
Seizures may be present or absent; if present, documented seizure history/EEG supports diagnosis
Exclude common mimics suggested by symptoms (e.g., central diabetes insipidus suggested by polyuria/polydipsia with hypernatremia) using available labs

expected_lab_results.Comprehensive metabolic panel (Sodium): Typically normal (no persistent hypernatremia to suggest untreated central diabetes insipidus)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive metabolic panel (BUN): Typically within reference range (may be elevated if dehydration from suspected DI, but not expected in baseline genetic syndrome)

medications: No disease-specific pharmacotherapy; treat comorbidities symptomatically (e.g., antiseizure medication if seizures are present per neurology)
Melatonin 1-3 mg PO nightly PRN sleep-onset insomnia (if clinically indicated)
non_pharmacological: Referral to clinical genetics for confirmatory testing (gene panel/exome) and genetic counseling (autosomal dominant inheritance, recurrence risk, family testing options)
Neurology referral for ataxia evaluation and seizure screening; obtain EEG if seizure concern; brain MRI if indicated by neurology for progressive ataxia, focal deficits, or atypical features
Developmental/behavioral interventions: early intervention/special education supports; speech-language therapy; occupational therapy; physical therapy for ataxia and gait safety
Behavioral health assessment for anxiety/behavioral dysregulation; implement parent/caregiver training and behavioral therapy (CBT adapted to developmental level when feasible)
Safety planning for falls (home safety evaluation, assistive devices as recommended by PT), and seizure safety counseling if seizures suspected/confirmed
Screen for obesity and provide nutrition/activity counseling; consider dietitian referral if overweight/obesity present

If symptoms suggest central diabetes insipidus (polyuria/polydipsia, nocturia, dehydration), initiate focused evaluation (serum sodium/BUN trends; add serum/urine osmolality and urine specific gravity when available; endocrinology referral)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-6 weeks to review genetic/neurology referrals and therapy initiation; sooner (1-2 weeks) if rapid functional decline, dehydration symptoms, or new seizures

must_include: This is a neurodevelopmental genetic condition characterized by intellectual disability/developmental delay, behavioral differences, and distinctive facial features; seizures may or may not occur
Autosomal dominant inheritance meaning a single altered gene copy can cause the condition; discuss possibility of de novo vs inherited variant
Ataxia is part of the neurologic features and warrants neurology evaluation and supportive therapies
Labs are not diagnostic for the genetic syndrome; they help rule out treatable metabolic causes (e.g., dehydration/hypernatremia suggesting diabetes insipidus)
No curative medication; care focuses on symptom management and maximizing function
Key components: genetics confirmation/counseling, PT/OT/speech, behavioral supports, and neurology evaluation for seizures/ataxia
If seizures are present, antiseizure therapy is individualized by neurology; emphasize adherence and monitoring if started
Discuss what to do if new seizures, worsening gait, or signs of dehydration occur (seek urgent care)
Discuss prognosis as variable; emphasize developmental progress with supports and the need for ongoing services
Review family planning implications and offer testing for parents/siblings when appropriate
Acknowledge caregiver stress and offer resources (social work, community supports, school accommodations)
Fall-prevention strategies and safe physical activity tailored to ataxia
Sleep hygiene strategies; if using melatonin, discuss dosing, timing, and to avoid combining with other sedatives without guidance
Healthy nutrition and activity to reduce obesity risk
If polyuria/polydipsia develops, encourage prompt evaluation rather than fluid restriction

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting concerns and baseline: developmental history, behavior, craniofacial concerns, seizures/episodes, gait/ataxia, hydration/polyuria-polydipsia, sleep, meds/supplements, comorbidities, family history, safety risks.

Order screening labs to rule out treatable contributors and assess dehydration/electrolyte issues (not to diagnose the genetic syndrome): BMP/CMP (Na, K, glucose, BUN/Cr), serum osmolality if available, urinalysis and urine osmolality/specific gravity if polyuria/polydipsia, CBC and TSH as clinically indicated.

Review labs to identify urgent abnormalities (e.g., hypernatremia, hyperglycemia, renal dysfunction) that require prompt action and to support counseling that labs are not diagnostic for the syndrome.

Synthesize clinical picture consistent with intellectual developmental disorder with behavioral abnormalities and craniofacial dysmorphism with or without seizures; document differential and need for genetics confirmation; identify red flags needing urgent referral (new seizures, acute worsening ataxia, dehydration).

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, inheritance/de novo vs inherited, role of ataxia and neurology/PT/OT, clarify labs’ role; explain treatment approach and urgent return precautions; address prognosis variability, family planning/testing, caregiver stress/resources; lifestyle education including fall prevention, sleep hygiene/melatonin guidance, nutrition/obesity prevention, and prompt evaluation for polyuria/polydipsia.
Arrange follow-up and referrals: genetics counseling/testing, neurology (seizures/ataxia), PT/OT/speech, behavioral therapy, social work/school supports; plan monitoring and reassessment.
Required critical safety check before any prescribing (including melatonin or antiseizure meds if initiated).
Noncritical but expected before prescribing to reduce adverse events (review current meds/supplements and potential sedative interactions).

If prescribing symptomatic therapy (e.g., melatonin for sleep, or antiseizure medication per neurology plan), ensure allergy and interaction checks completed and provide dosing/monitoring instructions.
Close encounter with clear plan, safety-net instructions, and follow-up.

patient.chief_complaint: My feet and ankles feel weak, and I keep tripping—it's been slowly getting worse.

volunteer: My feet and ankles feel weak, and I keep tripping—it's been slowly getting worse.
I’ve always been kind of clumsy, but lately it’s more noticeable.
My shoes wear out weirdly and I feel like my feet don’t sit flat in them.
I get tired faster when I walk longer distances.
if_asked: I sometimes catch my toes on the ground like my foot doesn’t lift enough.
My ankles feel like they roll easily, especially on uneven ground.
My calves look thinner than they used to, even though the rest of me hasn’t changed much.
I get pins-and-needles or a numb feeling in my toes sometimes.
My hands feel weaker for things like opening jars or buttoning small buttons.
This runs in my family, but it seems to affect people differently—some relatives barely have issues and others need braces.
I’ve had these issues since my teens, but it’s been slowly progressing.
hidden: I’ve been getting more calluses and sore spots on my feet because I don’t always notice pressure until it’s painful.
I’ve started avoiding stairs or dark hallways because I don’t trust my footing.
I sometimes don’t feel small cuts or blisters on my toes until I see them.
I’ve been switching to wider shoes because my toes feel crowded or curled.
resistant: I’ve had a couple of falls in public and it’s embarrassing, so I’ve been avoiding social outings that involve walking.
I’ve been worried people think I’m drunk because I can look unsteady sometimes.
misleading: I thought it might be a pinched nerve in my back because my feet tingle sometimes.
I wondered if it’s just bad circulation because my feet can feel cold and kind of numb.
noise: I’ve had some mild headaches lately from stress at work.
My seasonal allergies have been acting up with a stuffy nose and sneezing.

general_concerns: I thought Charcot-Marie-Tooth is basically muscular dystrophy and that I’ll end up in a wheelchair soon no matter what.
Since my symptoms are mild compared to my dad’s, I’m not sure it’s really the same disease or if I was misdiagnosed.
If I start taking betamethasone, it should reduce the nerve inflammation and stop the weakness from progressing.
If I exercise hard enough and push through fatigue, I can rebuild the nerves and reverse the foot drop.
If genetic testing confirms it, that means my children will definitely have it and it will be severe.
economic_concerns: I’m worried braces/orthotics and physical therapy will be considered ‘optional’ and won’t be covered, so I might have to skip them.
If I tell my employer or insurance about a genetic condition, I could lose coverage or be denied future benefits.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: your feet/ankles feel weak, you keep tripping, and it’s slowly getting worse. Volunteer that you’ve always been a bit clumsy but it’s more noticeable now, your shoes wear out oddly, your feet don’t feel flat in them, and you tire faster on longer walks.

If asked, describe toe-catching/foot not lifting, ankles rolling on uneven ground, thinner calves, occasional pins-and-needles/numb toes, mild hand weakness (jars/buttons), and that it runs in your family with variable severity; symptoms began in your teens and have progressed slowly.

Do not mention unless specifically asked about skin/foot care or safety: more calluses/sore spots, not noticing pressure/cuts/blisters, avoiding stairs/dark hallways, switching to wider shoes due to curled/crowded toes.

Only admit if asked about social impact: falls are embarrassing; you avoid outings and worry people think you’re drunk.

Express misconceptions: you wondered about a pinched back nerve or poor circulation because feet can tingle/cold.

If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge relief and share concerns about coverage for braces/PT and fear of genetic-label insurance/employment consequences.

confounders: Prediabetes with possible early peripheral neuropathy symptoms (paresthesias) that can mimic/worsen hereditary neuropathy

Intermittent topical corticosteroid use; systemic steroid exposure (if used) could transiently affect glucose and contribute to proximal weakness, confusing symptom pattern

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy (HMSN)
CMT1A (PMP22 duplication) if genetic subtype specified

required_evidence: Length-dependent peripheral neuropathy pattern with distal weakness/atrophy (e.g., foot drop) and sensory loss
Pes cavus/high arches and/or hammer toes; gait instability
Reduced/absent ankle reflexes
Slowly progressive course with onset often in childhood/adolescence or early adulthood; variable expressivity within family
Family history of similar neuropathy/foot deformities (may be absent due to de novo mutation/variable penetrance)
Electrodiagnostic evidence consistent with hereditary neuropathy (demyelinating slowed conduction velocities vs axonal reduced amplitudes)
Exclusion of common acquired neuropathy causes (e.g., diabetes, B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism) based on history/targeted testing as indicated
